blocks.content: When a request to the Vesicash API cannot be completed, the API returns an error response. All errors follow a consistent JSON structure designed to be easy to parse and handle programmatically.
Vesicash uses standard HTTP status codes to indicate the outcome of every request:

cells.text: **Created** — A new resource was created successfully.
**Bad Request** — The request is malformed or missing required parameters. Check your request body and query string.
**Unauthorised** — Your API key is missing, invalid, or has been revoked. Re-check your `Authorization` header.
**Forbidden** — Your key is valid but lacks permission for this action (e.g. using a public key where a secret key is required).
**Not Found** — The requested resource does not exist. Check the reference or ID you supplied.
**Unprocessable Entity** — The request is well-formed but the values fail validation (e.g. an unsupported currency code).
**Too Many Requests** — You have exceeded the rate limit. Read the `Retry-After` header and back off before retrying.
**Server Error** — Something went wrong on Vesicash's end. These are rare and are monitored automatically.

blocks.content: Every error response uses the same envelope as a success response, with `"status": "error"`:

rows.name: code
rows.description: The HTTP status code, mirrored in the body for convenience

rows.name: message
rows.description: A human-readable description of what went wrong

rows.name: data
rows.description: `null` for most errors; may contain field-level validation detail for `422` responses

blocks.content: For `400` and `422` responses, the `data` field may contain a map of field-level errors:
Each key in `data` corresponds to the name of the failing field; each value is an array of error strings explaining what went wrong.
Vesicash errors fall into three broad categories:

cells.text: Something went wrong on Vesicash's infrastructure. Retry with exponential backoff.
A required field is missing, the wrong type, or fails a constraint. Fix the request before retrying.
The request was valid but the payment processor or banking partner declined it (e.g. insufficient funds, card blocked). Do not auto-retry without customer action.

props.language: @callout type="warning" title="Always check status in the response body"

blocks.content: Never rely solely on the HTTP status code. Some gateways or proxies may return `200` with an error body — always read `status` from the response envelope to confirm the outcome.

- **Show `message` to your support team, not to end users.** Error messages are technical. Display your own user-friendly copy in your UI.
- **Log `code` + `message` + the request reference together** to make errors searchable in your logging system.
- **Don't auto-retry `4xx` errors.** Client errors mean the request itself is wrong — retrying the same request produces the same error.
- **Retry `5xx` errors with exponential backoff.** Server errors are transient. Wait 1s, then 2s, 4s, 8s — give up after 4–5 attempts.
